age: 67. gender: M.

tPA adminsistration

 
History of Present Illness:
___ year old man with a history ESRD, on HD ___, DM, HTN, 
CAD s/p IMI with DES in ___ recent stress shows reversible 
defect in inferior wall but overall improved per cardiologist. 
Complained of cough and left sided chest pain, he was then 
having a cough productive of gray and white sputum, he did have 
drenching night sweats, unable to tell if he had weight loss. 
Followed for latent TB infection by Dr. ___ been treated 
with INH, but while being treated was exposed to his son so 
there was a question of whether he could be treated or not. Dr. 
___ feel like he was adequetely treated for TB. The CP was 
constant, sharp, and pleuritic, made worse with climbing stairs, 
similar to angina, EKG showed LVH with early repol abnormality, 
no signs of acute ischemia, was ruled out for MI. With cough, 
night sweats, questionable history of TB, decided he needed to 
be ruled out for TB. On CXR had a LLL infiltrate. Was in sbps in 
180s upon arrival, gave his home meds because they didn't think 
this was urgency and he was 160 systolic prior to HS. They were 
going to start CTX/Azithro after HD the next morning. Around 
___ in AM at 8am, intern spoke with him via interpreter, said 
his CP was improved, and his headache was improved (longstanding 
and worse on HD days), and so at that time moving all his 
extremities, no focal deficits, then had bedside HD after exam, 
and nurse noted that he was moving his extremities, then came in 
around 9:20 for AM rounds found to have flaccid LUE paralysis, 
left nasal-labial fold flattening. CODE STROKE called, taken to 
head CT, showed right MCA perfusion defect with "penumbra", but 
no evidence of bleed. BP was 210 systolic. So he was admitted to 
the ___ and started TPA. Wheeled him to the MICU but was 
turned away because no respiration bed. Gave 10 x 2 brought his 
BP down to 177 systpolic, at 1115 pushed TPA on the floor, neuro 
attending at bedside, pushed continuous infusion. By the time 
back to room, still aphasic but starting to move his LUE before 
starting TPA. Then confirmed FULL CODE with family, transferrred 
to ___ under neuro team. Remains aphasic and still has LUE 
weakness, but is moving it, still has nasolabial fold flattening 
on the left. They did see a R carotid stenosis. No a fib on EKG.
  
In Neuro ICU, patient started to speak slowly, so hopefully more 
dysarthria as opposed to enunciating rather than true aphasia. 
Put the order in for an MRI given his cypher stent, but he has 
an unusual intracranial circulation, he is a vasculopath, has 
all the risk factors for large vessel disease, ?embolic 
phenomena, so getting a TTE with bubble study. But now anxious 
and associating HD with stroke unfortunately, need to reiterate 
that with him. Currently, 98  73  147/56 (required 10 of 
labetolol and hydralazine IV this morning for 170s)  15  982L. 
He did pass speech and swallow. He was transferred to the 
medical floor for continued stabilization before discharge to 
home with ___.
 
Past Medical History:
ESRD -CKD stage 5 on hemodialysis.  
DM2 
CAD s/p drug-eluting stent ___  
Hyperlipidemia  
HTN  
Anemia
Latent TB treated with INH ___  
Chronic HA

obesity, and fatty liver

HEART: RRR, ___ continuous systolic-diastolic murmur, no rubs or

ABDOMEN:  +BS, soft, NT/ND, no guarding
EXTREMITIES: WWP, thrill at LUE AV fistula, slightly more 
swollen L arm without pitting edema or rubor/calor/dolor, no

NEURO: Awake, speaking in short sentences, persistent left NLF 
droop, smile asymmetric with drooping on L side, symmetric 
eyebrow raise and palate elevation, Strength 4+/5 and equal b/l 
and distally. LT intact bilaterally. RAM continues to be slowed 
on left compared to right. Can ambulate to bathroom without 
assistance.

# ___ EKG:
Normal sinus rhythm at a rate of 80. Right bundle-branch block. 
Mild 
prolongation of the Q-T interval. Left ventricular hypertrophy. 
There are 
diffuse non-specific ST-T wave changes throughout. Clinical 
correlation is 
suggested.  
  
# ___ CXR:
IMPRESSION: Mild pulmonary edema with bilateral small pleural 
effusions and atelectasis. 
  
# ___ EKG:
Normal sinus rhythm. Right bundle-branch block. Non-specific 
ST-T wave 
changes, all of which are unchanged compared with the previous 
tracing. 
  
# ___ CT Brain Perfusion
IMPRESSION: 
1. No acute intra- or extra-axial hemorrhage. 
2. Relatively small geographic region of abnormally increased 
mean transit 
time, without definite corresponding decreased relative cerebral 
blood volume, 
in the superior right frontal lobe, representing acute ischemic 
(penumbra) in 
the superior division, right MCA territory; there is no similar 
region 
elsewhere. 
3. Unremarkable intracranial circulation; specifically, no 
stenosis or 
occlusion of the right MCA and its branches or the right ICA 
terminus. 
4. Atherosclerotic disease, involving particularly the left 
carotid bulb and 
proximal ICA with at least 65% diameter stenosis at its origin. 
5. Evidence of CHF with bilateral pleural effusions. 
  
# ___ TTE
Conclusion:
The left atrium is moderately dilated. There is mild symmetric 
left ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size and 
regional/global systolic function (LVEF>55%). Doppler parameters 
are indeterminate for left ventricular diastolic function. There 
is no ventricular septal defect. Right ventricular chamber size 
and free wall motion are normal. The aortic root is mildly 
dilated at the sinus level. The aortic valve leaflets (3) are 
mildly thickened but aortic stenosis is not present. No aortic 
regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly 
thickened. There is no mitral valve prolapse. The tricuspid 
valve leaflets are mildly thickened. There is borderline 
pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is a very small 
pericardial effusion. 
  
IMPRESSION: Mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with 
preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function. 
Indeterminate indices to assess diastolic dysfunction. No 
pathologic valvular abnormality seen.
  
# ___ CXR:
FINDINGS: In comparison with the study of ___, there is 
increasing 
opacification at both bases with poor definition of the 
hemidiaphragms 
consistent with pleural effusions and compressive atelectasis. 
The degree of pulmonary vascular congestion is more prominent, 
though some of this could reflect the AP supine rather than PA 
technique. 
  
# ___ Non Con Head CT
IMPRESSION: 
Residual IV contrast limits optimal evaluation for subtle 
hemorrhage.  
1. New focal hypodense area with loss of gray-white 
differentiation in the 
right frontal lobe corresponding to the previously seen 
perfusion abnormality representing either evolving ischemia or 
infarction.  
2. No large area of hemorrhage in this area or elsewhere within 
limitation of this study. No new edema, mass effect, or shift in 
midline structures. 
  
# ___ TTE Bubble Study
Findings:
No evidence suggestive of an atrial septal defect or patent 
foramen ovale is seen by 2D, color Doppler or saline contrast 
with maneuvers.  
   
# ___ MRI Head
IMPRESSION: 
  
1. Acute infarct in the right posterior frontal lobe involving 
the precentral gyrus which is unchanged. 
   
2. No evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage or new infarct. 
   
3. Changes of chronic small vessel ischemic disease. 
   
4. Arachnoid cyst versus a prominent cisterna magna in the 
posterior fossa.  
  

  

 
Brief Hospital Course:
ASSESSMENT:  ___ yo ___ RHM with h/o ESRD, CAD s/p 
drug-eluting stent placed ___, DM2, HTN, HL, and latent TB 
initially presented on ___ with c/o CP, productive cough and 
night sweats, admitted for MI rule out hospital course c/b 
right-sided acute cerebral infarct s/p tPA on ___, initially 
with motor weakness, left sided facial droop and expressive 
aphasia, now with symmetric strength and no facial droop but 
persistent expressive aphasia. 
  
# Acute Cerebral Infarct: While in dialysis on ___ pt was 
found around 09:20 to have new speech difficulty and LUE flaccid 
weakness. A Code Stroke was called and CTA showed small 
frontoparietal increased MTT/perfusion deficit. He was 
administered tPA starting at approximately 11:15 on ___. He was 
then transfered to the TICU for frequent neuro checks given risk 
of bleed post-tPA. Passed speech and swallow on ___. 
Continued to improve on the medical floor, with equal strength 
and sensation distally. TTE showed no ASD or PFO. Cardiology had 
low suspicion for any left atrial thrombus or appendage 
abnormality, so TEE was recommended for outpatient. Neuro aware 
of left ICA. LDL is 95, so started a statin to obtain less than 
70. Followed daily by neurology. Antihypertensives were 
uptitrated as necessary to bring BP within goal SBP 140-180.  
Patient was placed on Aspirin 325 mg per neuro recs because they 
felt plavix was not a good medication for him.
Treatment Course:
- Captopril 12.5 mg PO TID x1 day
- Captopril 37.5 mg PO TID x3 days
- Lisinopril 40 mg PO DAILY x2 days
- Metoprolol tartrate 25 mg PO BID x3 days
- Carvedilol 25 mg PO BID x3 days
- Hydralazine ___ mg IV Q6H PRN SBP>180
- Atorvastatin 80 mg daily x6 days
- Aspirin 325 mg PO/NG DAILY x7 days 
- Frequent neuro checks with monitoring for headache as 
indication of possible hemorrhagic conversion or cerebral edema
- Continuous monitoring by telemetry   
- Maintained normothermia  
- Risk Factor Assessment with Lipid panel 
  
# Headache: pt has h/o chronic HA with HD along with dizziness 
and ear ache. ___ have h/o migraine but due to language barrier 
it is described simply as HA. Associated with nausea and 
vomiting. Varies from ___ to ___ in severity.
- Acetaminophen dose to 1000 mg PO Q6H prn pain
- Ibuprofen 600 mg PO Q8H prn pain
- Odansetron 4 mg IV Q8H:PRN nausea/vomiting 
- Elevated HOB to 30 degrees
- Monitored closely for concurrent neurological findings 
- Pt sent with a prescription for Tramadol for break through 
headache pain
  
# No TB infection, ruled out after neg AFBx3 and neg 
concentrated smear. Has been followed for latent TB infection by 
Dr. ___ previously treated with INH. Resolved.
  
# ESRD: has a LUE fistula, after TPA did have more swelling of 
his LUE ?less mobile, non pitting edema, so don't believe there 
is a clot but something to monitor as an outpatient.  Dialysis 
on T, Tr, ___. 
- Monitored by daily Cr, urine output, electrolytes  
  
# No current chest pain on discharge, ruled out for MI with flat 
CK-MB and TpnT.  Resolved.
  
# Pulmonary Edema: Likely ___ fluid overload on admission CXR, 
now resolved.  Sats stable, lung sounds clear.
  
# DM 2: 
- Pt placed on Insulin SS with goal <150.  BG very well 
controlled during this admission.
  
# Anxiety: Pt has increased anxiety following stroke, especially 
around HD, now resolved.
- Pt given Lorazepam 0.5 mg IV PRN anxiety pre-HD.
     
# Consults:
- ___ evaluations for walking stairs, showering without assitance
- Nutrition evaluations for poor PO intake, treated with 
appetite stimulant Megace
- Social work consults for discharge home with services
  
Transitional Issues:
1. Pt still needs a TEE for a better look at possible atrial 
pathology which may have caused this stroke.  Cardiology did not 
feel that this procedure had to be done in house and that he was 
very low risk for having anything that would change our 
management.  For this reason he was set up with cardiology as an 
outpatient to get this TEE.
2. Pt will need close neurology follow up to follow neuro 
deficits and continue to reduce his risk of further stroke.
3. Pt will need close PCP follow up to follow up on new 
medications, medication refills, ongoing neuro deficits, stroke 
risk reduction etc.

Discharge Condition:

Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ___,
  
You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain which has 
resolved.  Unfortunately, while you were here you had a stroke 
that affected the right side of your brain (and consequently the 
left side of your body). You were given a medication that helps 
to dissolve clots and helps people improve following a stroke.  
Over the few days following the stroke you improved dramatically 
and your motor muscle function appears to be back to baseline.  
As we discussed, the reason for the stroke was secondary to 
years of high cholesterol, diabetes, high blood pressure, and 
dialysis that lead to hardening of the arteries, leading to a 
blockage. The key to your recovery lies in your new medications 
including aspirin, atorvastatin, and new blood pressure agents. 
Further, you will benefit from outpatient physical therapy, 
occupational therapy, and speech therapy. We have set you up 
with primary care, cardiology and neurology appointments below. 
    
Additionally, while you were here we tested you for tuberculosis 
and all of our tests were negative, indicating that you do not 
have this infection.
  
The following changes were made to your medications:
STOP Metoprolol
STOP Fluticasone
CHANGE Vitamin B complex vitamins to B complex with Vitamin C 
and Folic Acid, 1 capsule by mouth daily
CHANGE Aspirin from 81 mg daily to 325 mg daily
START Atorvastatin 80 mg by mouth once daily
START Carvedilol 25 mg by mouth twice daily
START Lisinopril 20 mg by mouth twice daily
START Megace 600 mg by mouth once daily
START Tramadol 50 mg by mouth every 6 hours as needed for 
headache
  
Thank you for allowing us to participate in your care.  We wish 
you a speedy recovery.
 
Followup Instructions:
___
 

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking:
The student's answer is straightforward and follows a normal flow to summarize the patient's clinical course and diagnostic findings. There are no alternative possibilities or backtracking in reasoning; hence, all steps should be retained as presented. Additionally, I will add '
' to split each reasoning step to aid in grading.
response:
Patient Summary: A 67-year-old male with ESRD on hemodialysis, CAD (s/p DES), DM2, and hypertension was admitted for chest pain and evaluation for active tuberculosis (TB). 
 His course was significant for an acute right MCA ischemic stroke occurring during hemodialysis on hospital day 2, for which he received intravenous tPA.
 
Key Diagnostic Results:
 - Neurology: Brain CT perfusion and follow-up MRI confirmed an acute infarct in the right posterior frontal lobe (precentral gyrus) with no intracranial hemorrhage. 
 CTA revealed generalized atherosclerotic disease and 65% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery.
 - Cardiology: Myocardial infarction was ruled out with serial troponins and flat CK-MB. 
 Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE) demonstrated a preserved LVEF (>55%) and mild LVH; a saline bubble study was negative for ASD or PFO.
 - Infectious Disease: Active TB was ruled out via three negative sputum Acid-Fast Bacilli (AFB) smears and cultures.
 - Laboratory/Imaging: Baseline creatinine (5.9–10.9 mg/dL) and mild anemia (Hgb 10.6–12.2 g/dL) were consistent with ESRD. 
 Initial pulmonary edema and pleural effusions seen on CXR resolved with fluid management.
 
Clinical Outcome and Management: Following tPA, the patient showed significant improvement, progressing from flaccid left-sided paralysis to 4+/5 motor strength bilaterally.
 At discharge, he remained alert with mild expressive aphasia and left facial drooping.
 Management focused on secondary stroke prevention (Aspirin 325mg, Atorvastatin 80mg) and blood pressure control (Lisinopril, Carvedilol).
 He was discharged home in stable condition, ambulatory with assistance, with orders for outpatient Physical, Occupational, and Speech Therapy, and a scheduled outpatient TEE.
candidate:
The patient is a 67-year-old male with multiple comorbidities including ESRD, CAD, DM2, and HTN, initially admitted for chest pain and TB rule-out. His hospital course was complicated by an acute right MCA ischemic stroke occurring on Day 2 during hemodialysis. The core task is to summarize the diagnostic findings and clinical results. Key diagnostics include Brain CT/MRI (confirming right frontal infarct), Carotid CTA (showing 65% left ICA stenosis), TTE/Bubble study (normal EF, no ASD/PFO), and negative AFB/Cardiac markers. The outcome was significant neurological recovery following tPA, though mild aphasia and facial droop remained at discharge. Discharge plans involve secondary stroke prevention and outpatient therapy.

Answer:Patient Summary: A 67-year-old male with ESRD on hemodialysis, CAD (s/p DES), DM2, and hypertension was admitted for chest pain and evaluation for active tuberculosis (TB). His course was significant for an acute right MCA ischemic stroke occurring during hemodialysis on hospital day 2, for which he received intravenous tPA.

Key Diagnostic Results:
- Neurology: Brain CT perfusion and follow-up MRI confirmed an acute infarct in the right posterior frontal lobe (precentral gyrus) with no intracranial hemorrhage. CTA revealed generalized atherosclerotic disease and 65% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery.
- Cardiology: Myocardial infarction was ruled out with serial troponins and flat CK-MB. Transthoracic Echocardiogram (TTE) demonstrated a preserved LVEF (>55%) and mild LVH; a saline bubble study was negative for ASD or PFO.
- Infectious Disease: Active TB was ruled out via three negative sputum Acid-Fast Bacilli (AFB) smears and cultures.
- Laboratory/Imaging: Baseline creatinine (5.9–10.9 mg/dL) and mild anemia (Hgb 10.6–12.2 g/dL) were consistent with ESRD. Initial pulmonary edema and pleural effusions seen on CXR resolved with fluid management.

Clinical Outcome and Management: Following tPA, the patient showed significant improvement, progressing from flaccid left-sided paralysis to 4+/5 motor strength bilaterally. At discharge, he remained alert with mild expressive aphasia and left facial drooping. Management focused on secondary stroke prevention (Aspirin 325mg, Atorvastatin 80mg) and blood pressure control (Lisinopril, Carvedilol). He was discharged home in stable condition, ambulatory with assistance, with orders for outpatient Physical, Occupational, and Speech Therapy, and a scheduled outpatient TEE.

In Neuro ICU, patient started to speak slowly, so hopefully more

# ___ EKG:
Normal sinus rhythm at a rate of 80. Right bundle-branch block.

suggested.

effusions and atelectasis. 
  
# ___ EKG:
Normal sinus rhythm. Right bundle-branch block. Non-specific 
ST-T wave 
changes, all of which are unchanged compared with the previous 
tracing. 
  
# ___ CT Brain Perfusion
IMPRESSION: 
1. No acute intra- or extra-axial hemorrhage. 
2. Relatively small geographic region of abnormally increased 
mean transit 
time, without definite corresponding decreased relative cerebral 
blood volume, 
in the superior right frontal lobe, representing acute ischemic 
(penumbra) in 
the superior division, right MCA territory; there is no similar 
region 
elsewhere. 
3. Unremarkable intracranial circulation; specifically, no 
stenosis or 
occlusion of the right MCA and its branches or the right ICA 
terminus. 
4. Atherosclerotic disease, involving particularly the left 
carotid bulb and 
proximal ICA with at least 65% diameter stenosis at its origin. 
5. Evidence of CHF with bilateral pleural effusions. 
  
# ___ TTE
Conclusion:
The left atrium is moderately dilated. There is mild symmetric 
left ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size and 
regional/global systolic function (LVEF>55%). Doppler parameters 
are indeterminate for left ventricular diastolic function. There 
is no ventricular septal defect. Right ventricular chamber size 
and free wall motion are normal. The aortic root is mildly 
dilated at the sinus level. The aortic valve leaflets (3) are 
mildly thickened but aortic stenosis is not present. No aortic 
regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve leaflets are mildly 
thickened. There is no mitral valve prolapse. The tricuspid 
valve leaflets are mildly thickened. There is borderline 
pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is a very small 
pericardial effusion. 
  
IMPRESSION: Mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with 
preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function. 
Indeterminate indices to assess diastolic dysfunction. No 
pathologic valvular abnormality seen.
  
# ___ CXR:
FINDINGS: In comparison with the study of ___, there is 
increasing 
opacification at both bases with poor definition of the 
hemidiaphragms 
consistent with pleural effusions and compressive atelectasis. 
The degree of pulmonary vascular congestion is more prominent, 
though some of this could reflect the AP supine rather than PA 
technique. 
  
# ___ Non Con Head CT
IMPRESSION: 
Residual IV contrast limits optimal evaluation for subtle 
hemorrhage.  
1. New focal hypodense area with loss of gray-white 
differentiation in the 
right frontal lobe corresponding to the previously seen 
perfusion abnormality representing either evolving ischemia or 
infarction.  
2. No large area of hemorrhage in this area or elsewhere within 
limitation of this study. No new edema, mass effect, or shift in 
midline structures. 
  
# ___ TTE Bubble Study
Findings:
No evidence suggestive of an atrial septal defect or patent 
foramen ovale is seen by 2D, color Doppler or saline contrast 
with maneuvers.  
   
# ___ MRI Head
IMPRESSION: 
  
1. Acute infarct in the right posterior frontal lobe involving 
the precentral gyrus which is unchanged. 
   
2. No evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage or new infarct. 
   
3. Changes of chronic small vessel ischemic disease. 
   
4. Arachnoid cyst versus a prominent cisterna magna in the 
posterior fossa.  
  

  

 
Brief Hospital Course:
ASSESSMENT:  ___ yo ___ RHM with h/o ESRD, CAD s/p 
drug-eluting stent placed ___, DM2, HTN, HL, and latent TB 
initially presented on ___ with c/o CP, productive cough and 
night sweats, admitted for MI rule out hospital course c/b 
right-sided acute cerebral infarct s/p tPA on ___, initially 
with motor weakness, left sided facial droop and expressive 
aphasia, now with symmetric strength and no facial droop but 
persistent expressive aphasia. 
  
# Acute Cerebral Infarct: While in dialysis on ___ pt was 
found around 09:20 to have new speech difficulty and LUE flaccid 
weakness. A Code Stroke was called and CTA showed small 
frontoparietal increased MTT/perfusion deficit. He was 
administered tPA starting at approximately 11:15 on ___. He was 
then transfered to the TICU for frequent neuro checks given risk 
of bleed post-tPA. Passed speech and swallow on ___. 
Continued to improve on the medical floor, with equal strength 
and sensation distally. TTE showed no ASD or PFO. Cardiology had 
low suspicion for any left atrial thrombus or appendage 
abnormality, so TEE was recommended for outpatient. Neuro aware 
of left ICA. LDL is 95, so started a statin to obtain less than 
70. Followed daily by neurology. Antihypertensives were 
uptitrated as necessary to bring BP within goal SBP 140-180.  
Patient was placed on Aspirin 325 mg per neuro recs because they 
felt plavix was not a good medication for him.
Treatment Course:
- Captopril 12.5 mg PO TID x1 day
- Captopril 37.5 mg PO TID x3 days
- Lisinopril 40 mg PO DAILY x2 days
- Metoprolol tartrate 25 mg PO BID x3 days
- Carvedilol 25 mg PO BID x3 days
- Hydralazine ___ mg IV Q6H PRN SBP>180
- Atorvastatin 80 mg daily x6 days
- Aspirin 325 mg PO/NG DAILY x7 days 
- Frequent neuro checks with monitoring for headache as 
indication of possible hemorrhagic conversion or cerebral edema
- Continuous monitoring by telemetry   
- Maintained normothermia  
- Risk Factor Assessment with Lipid panel 
  
# Headache: pt has h/o chronic HA with HD along with dizziness 
and ear ache. ___ have h/o migraine but due to language barrier 
it is described simply as HA. Associated with nausea and 
vomiting. Varies from ___ to ___ in severity.
- Acetaminophen dose to 1000 mg PO Q6H prn pain
- Ibuprofen 600 mg PO Q8H prn pain
- Odansetron 4 mg IV Q8H:PRN nausea/vomiting 
- Elevated HOB to 30 degrees
- Monitored closely for concurrent neurological findings 
- Pt sent with a prescription for Tramadol for break through 
headache pain
  
# No TB infection, ruled out after neg AFBx3 and neg 
concentrated smear. Has been followed for latent TB infection by 
Dr. ___ previously treated with INH. Resolved.
  
# ESRD: has a LUE fistula, after TPA did have more swelling of 
his LUE ?less mobile, non pitting edema, so don't believe there 
is a clot but something to monitor as an outpatient.  Dialysis 
on T, Tr, ___. 
- Monitored by daily Cr, urine output, electrolytes  
  
# No current chest pain on discharge, ruled out for MI with flat 
CK-MB and TpnT.  Resolved.
  
# Pulmonary Edema: Likely ___ fluid overload on admission CXR, 
now resolved.  Sats stable, lung sounds clear.
  
# DM 2: 
- Pt placed on Insulin SS with goal <150.  BG very well 
controlled during this admission.
  
# Anxiety: Pt has increased anxiety following stroke, especially 
around HD, now resolved.
- Pt given Lorazepam 0.5 mg IV PRN anxiety pre-HD.
     
# Consults:
- ___ evaluations for walking stairs, showering without assitance
- Nutrition evaluations for poor PO intake, treated with 
appetite stimulant Megace
- Social work consults for discharge home with services
  
Transitional Issues:
1. Pt still needs a TEE for a better look at possible atrial 
pathology which may have caused this stroke.  Cardiology did not 
feel that this procedure had to be done in house and that he was 
very low risk for having anything that would change our 
management.  For this reason he was set up with cardiology as an 
outpatient to get this TEE.
2. Pt will need close neurology follow up to follow neuro 
deficits and continue to reduce his risk of further stroke.
3. Pt will need close PCP follow up to follow up on new 
medications, medication refills, ongoing neuro deficits, stroke 
risk reduction etc.

Discharge Condition:

Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ___,
  
You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain which has 
resolved.  Unfortunately, while you were here you had a stroke 
that affected the right side of your brain (and consequently the 
left side of your body). You were given a medication that helps 
to dissolve clots and helps people improve following a stroke.  
Over the few days following the stroke you improved dramatically 
and your motor muscle function appears to be back to baseline.  
As we discussed, the reason for the stroke was secondary to 
years of high cholesterol, diabetes, high blood pressure, and 
dialysis that lead to hardening of the arteries, leading to a 
blockage. The key to your recovery lies in your new medications 
including aspirin, atorvastatin, and new blood pressure agents. 
Further, you will benefit from outpatient physical therapy, 
occupational therapy, and speech therapy. We have set you up 
with primary care, cardiology and neurology appointments below. 
    
Additionally, while you were here we tested you for tuberculosis 
and all of our tests were negative, indicating that you do not 
have this infection.
  
The following changes were made to your medications:
STOP Metoprolol
STOP Fluticasone
CHANGE Vitamin B complex vitamins to B complex with Vitamin C 
and Folic Acid, 1 capsule by mouth daily
CHANGE Aspirin from 81 mg daily to 325 mg daily
START Atorvastatin 80 mg by mouth once daily
START Carvedilol 25 mg by mouth twice daily
START Lisinopril 20 mg by mouth twice daily
START Megace 600 mg by mouth once daily
START Tramadol 50 mg by mouth every 6 hours as needed for 
headache

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking: The student's answer is well-structured and follows a coherent flow of information. There is no evidence of backtracking or multiple possibilities in the reasoning. The answer presents a normal flow of a medical report, detailing significant diagnostic results sequentially and concluding with a comprehensive discharge summary. Each section builds upon the previous without contradictions, so all elements are retained. I will add '\n' to split each reasoning step.
response:
The patient is a 67-year-old male with a complex medical history including End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) on hemodialysis, Coronary Artery Disease (CAD), Diabetes Mellitus type 2 (DM2), and hypertension. He was admitted for chest pain and to rule out tuberculosis (TB) due to cough and night sweats. His hospital course was significantly complicated by an in-hospital acute ischemic stroke occurring during hemodialysis. This was managed with tPA. Diagnostic workup included extensive neuroimaging, cardiac evaluation, and microbiology for TB. The discharge summary consolidates these events, diagnostic results, and the treatment plan. 
 Significant Results: 
 - Laboratory Findings: Renal function tests were consistently abnormal, with Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN) peaking at 70 mg/dL and Creatinine at 10.9 mg/dL, consistent with ESRD. Hematology showed chronic anemia (Hemoglobin 10.6–12.2 g/dL) and episodes of mild leukopenia (WBC 3.6–3.7 K/uL). Blood glucose was elevated, peaking at 147 mg/dL. Electrolytes revealed hyperphosphatemia (up to 6.8 mg/dL) and an elevated anion gap (up to 25). Cardiac biomarkers (Troponin-T 0.04–0.05 ng/mL) remained flat, ruling out acute myocardial infarction. Microbiology: Three sputum Acid-Fast Bacilli (AFB) smears and cultures were negative, ruling out active TB. 
 - Imaging & Diagnostics: 
 1. CT Brain Perfusion: Showed right MCA territory ischemia (penumbra) in the superior right frontal lobe and 65% stenosis of the left proximal internal carotid artery (ICA). 
 2. Non-Contrast Head CT: Revealed new focal hypodensity in the right frontal lobe corresponding to evolving infarction. 
 3. MRI Head: Confirmed acute infarct in the right posterior frontal lobe (precentral gyrus) and chronic small vessel ischemic disease. 
 4. EKG: Showed Right Bundle Branch Block (RBBB) and Left Ventricular Hypertrophy (LVH). 
 5. TTE & Bubble Study: Preserved LVEF (>55%), moderate left atrial dilation, and no evidence of ASD or PFO. 
 6. Chest X-ray: Initially showed mild pulmonary edema, bilateral small pleural effusions, and atelectasis, which improved after dialysis. 
 Discharge Summary: 
 Patient: 67-year-old male. 
 Reason for Hospitalization: Initially admitted for pleuritic chest pain, productive cough, and night sweats (TB rule-out). 
 Hospital Course: On the second hospital day, during hemodialysis, the patient developed acute flaccid left upper extremity paralysis, facial droop, and aphasia. A 'Code Stroke' was triggered. CT imaging confirmed a right MCA territory ischemic penumbra. IV tPA was administered within the therapeutic window. He was monitored in the ICU and later transitioned to the floor. His motor strength significantly improved to 4+/5, and aphasia began to resolve. Active TB was ruled out via serial negative sputum studies. Chest pain and pulmonary edema were managed with optimized fluid removal during dialysis. 
 Discharge Status: The patient is ambulatory with the assistance of a walker/cane, mentally clear, and coherent. Left-sided facial droop and mild expressive aphasia persist but are improved. 
 Discharge Diagnoses: Acute Cerebral Infarct (Right MCA), ESRD on Hemodialysis, Type II Diabetes Mellitus, CAD, Chronic Anemia, and Hypertension. 
 Major Medications: Aspirin 325 mg daily, Atorvastatin 80 mg daily, Lisinopril 20 mg BID, Carvedilol 25 mg BID, and Tramadol (PRN for headache). 
 Follow-up: Outpatient TEE is required to further evaluate for embolic sources. Appointments are scheduled for Neurology, Cardiology, Primary Care, and outpatient Physical/Occupational/Speech therapy.
